title: Microeconomics with Mathematical Models — Mermaid-Safe v2
description: A rich microeconomics lesson for testing MathLesson rendering, export, quiz, and interactive blocks.

sections.label: §1 Model Thinking
sections.title: Model Thinking
sections.content:
Microeconomics with Mathematical Models

This lesson is a stress-test lesson. It uses microeconomic ideas to test prose, LaTeX, callouts, graphs, tables, grids, Mermaid, JSXGraph, exercises, quiz items, and exports.

Definition
A microeconomic model is a simplified mathematical description of individual choices, markets, firms, or strategic interaction.
Generic optimization form
Many microeconomic problems have the form
$$\max_{x\in X} f(x)\quad\text{subject to}\quad g_i(x)\leq b_i.$$
The function $f$ is the objective and the inequalities are constraints.

Consumers

Choose goods to maximize utility under a budget constraint.

$$\max U(x,y)$$

Firms

Choose output or inputs to maximize profit.

$$\max \pi(q)=R(q)-C(q)$$

Markets

Prices coordinate demand and supply.

$$Q_d(P)=Q_s(P)$$
Testing note
The equations are intentionally simple. The purpose is to verify that the app handles a mathematically rich lesson consistently across author view, student export, JSON export/import, Reveal export, and Print/PDF planning.

sections.label: §2 Supply and Demand
sections.title: Supply and Demand
sections.content:
Supply and Demand

A competitive market model compares the quantity buyers want with the quantity sellers want at each price.

Linear demand and supply
Let price be $P$. A simple model is
$$Q_d(P)=a-bP,\qquad Q_s(P)=c+dP,$$
where $b>0$ and $d>0$.
Equilibrium formula
The competitive equilibrium satisfies $Q_d(P^*)=Q_s(P^*)$. Thus
$$P^*=\frac{a-c}{b+d},\qquad Q^*=a-bP^*.$$
If $Q_d=80-4P$ and $Q_s=20+2P$, enter the equilibrium price $P^*$.

sections.label: §3 Consumer Choice
sections.title: Consumer Choice
sections.content:
Consumer Choice

Consumer theory models a household as choosing a bundle $(x,y)$ subject to a budget constraint.

Budget set
If income is $m$ and prices are $p_x,p_y$, then affordable bundles satisfy
$$p_xx+p_yy\leq m.$$
The budget line is $p_xx+p_yy=m$.
Cobb-Douglas utility
A common utility model is
$$U(x,y)=x^\alpha y^{1-\alpha},\qquad 0<\alpha<1.$$
The marginal rate of substitution is
$$MRS=\frac{MU_x}{MU_y}=\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}\frac{y}{x}.$$

Primal problem

Maximize utility subject to income.

$$\max U(x,y)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad p_xx+p_yy=m.$$

Result for Cobb-Douglas

The expenditure shares are fixed by $\alpha$.

$$x^*=\frac{\alpha m}{p_x},\qquad y^*=\frac{(1-\alpha)m}{p_y}.$$
For Cobb-Douglas utility, enter the Marshallian demand $x^*$.

sections.label: §4 Firm and Production
sections.title: Firm and Production
sections.content:
Firm and Production

A firm transforms inputs into output. A production function maps capital and labor into quantity produced.

Production function
A Cobb-Douglas production function is
$$q=A K^\alpha L^{1-\alpha}.$$
Here $K$ is capital, $L$ is labor, $A$ is productivity, and $0<\alpha<1$.
Profit maximization
If output price is $p$ and cost is $C(q)$, profit is
$$\pi(q)=pq-C(q).$$
An interior optimum satisfies
$$MR(q)=MC(q).$$
For a price-taking firm, $MR(q)=p$.
ConceptSymbolSimple formulaInterpretation
Total revenue$R(q)$$pq$money from sales
Total cost$C(q)$$F+cq+dq^2$fixed plus variable cost
Marginal cost$MC(q)$$C'(q)$cost of one more unit
Profit$\pi(q)$$R(q)-C(q)$firm objective

sections.label: §5 Elasticity
sections.title: Elasticity
sections.content:
Elasticity

Elasticity measures proportional sensitivity. It converts a slope into a unit-free response measure.

Price elasticity of demand
The point elasticity of demand is
$$\varepsilon(P)=\frac{dQ_d}{dP}\frac{P}{Q_d(P)}.$$
For $Q_d=a-bP$,
$$\varepsilon(P)=-b\frac{P}{a-bP}.$$
Interpretation
If $|\varepsilon|>1$, demand is elastic. If $|\varepsilon|<1$, demand is inelastic. The sign is usually negative for ordinary downward-sloping demand.
For $Q=100-5P$, enter elasticity at $P=10$.
Mock regression block

This placeholder block represents estimating a demand curve from price and quantity data.

sections.label: §6 Strategic Interaction
sections.title: Strategic Interaction
sections.content:
Strategic Interaction

Microeconomics also studies situations where one person’s payoff depends on another person’s action.

graph LR
  A["Output choice"] --> B["Market signal"]
  B --> C["Quantity choice"]
  C --> D["Price rule"]
  D --> E["Payoff"]
Firm B: LowFirm B: High
Firm A: Low3, 31, 4
Firm A: High4, 12, 2
Testing note
This section tests Mermaid-safe quoted labels, ordinary tables, and SVG rendering in the same section.

sections.label: §7 Export Layout and Practice
sections.title: Export Layout and Practice
sections.content:
Export Layout and Practice

This section includes layout controls and inline exercises for export testing.

Keep together
Equilibrium is the price-quantity pair where planned buying equals planned selling.
$$Q_d(P^*)=Q_s(P^*).$$

The comment above is a Reveal slide split marker.

This paragraph is inside an allow-break block. Repeat testing words: demand, supply, elasticity, utility, budget, production, profit, marginal cost, equilibrium, game, payoff, constraint, optimization.

This paragraph follows a forced Print/PDF page break.

ModelChoice variableObjectiveConstraintFirst-order condition
Consumer$x,y$$U(x,y)$$p_xx+p_yy=m$$MRS=p_x/p_y$
Firm$q$$pq-C(q)$technology$MR=MC$
Market$P$clearingdemand/supply$Q_d=Q_s$
Final prompt
After import, run Prototype Audit, Student export, JSON export/import, Reveal export, and Print/PDF Planner.

plots.supplyDemandPlot: (layout,target)=>{const P=Array.from({length:81},(_,i)=>i*0.25);const Qd=P.map(p=>80-4*p);const Qs=P.map(p=>20+2*p);const pe=10, qe=40;Plotly.newPlot(target,[{x:Qd,y:P,mode:'lines',name:'Demand Qd=80-4P'},{x:Qs,y:P,mode:'lines',name:'Supply Qs=20+2P'},{x:[qe],y:[pe],mode:'markers+text',text:['Equilibrium'],textposition:'top right',name:'E'}],{...layout,title:'Supply and demand stress-test plot',xaxis:{title:'quantity'},yaxis:{title:'price'},margin:{t:45,b:52,l:52,r:20}},{displayModeBar:false,responsive:true});}

quiz.stem: In a supply-demand model, equilibrium means:
quiz.feedback: Equilibrium clears the market: Qd equals Qs.

quiz.stem: For Qd(P)=a-bP with b>0, demand slopes downward.
quiz.feedback: As price rises, quantity demanded falls.

quiz.stem: If Qd=80-4P and Qs=20+2P, enter P*.

quiz.hint: Good x receives alpha share of income.

quiz.stemHtml: The condition $MR=MC$ is a firm optimization condition.
quiz.feedback: It is the usual interior optimum condition.

quiz.stem: Elasticity is useful because it is:
quiz.feedback: Elasticity measures proportional response and is unit-free.

quiz.stem: For Q=100-5P, enter epsilon at P=10.
Describe one model in the lesson and what its constraint represents.
quiz.feedback: A reasonable answer may discuss budget, technology, or market clearing constraints.
